findings.description: Inverted TTL check (`age < self.ttl` instead of `>=`) makes fresh L1 entries report as expired, so every read for the first ttl seconds skips the L1 hit, deletes the entry, and falls through to a Redis round-trip plus pickle.loads — the L1 cache has an effective 0% hit rate during its lifetime, turning the tiered cache into a Redis proxy with added deserialization overhead.

The L1 `_local` dict is unbounded — no max size, no LRU, no size-based eviction; combined with the inverted expiry it grows monotonically with keyspace cardinality and trends toward OOM in a long-running web service (needs LRUCache/TTLCache or a periodic sweep).

pickle.loads on the L2 read path is paid on every read for the first ttl seconds due to the expiry bug; pickle is slower/heavier than json/msgpack and its cost scales with object size, so large cached values incur real repeated CPU on the hot path (note: also a security/RCE concern across the Redis boundary — deferred to critic-security).

warm() calls set() per entry, issuing one synchronous Redis setex and one pickle.dumps per item with no pipelining/MSET — bulk warming of N entries costs N sequential network round-trips that a pipeline/batch write would collapse by orders of magnitude.

set() writes stats.local_entries = len(self._local) on every write, but export_stats (line 95) recomputes len(self._local) inline and the field is otherwise unread — dead write work on every set()/warm() iteration (O(1) each, so minor, but pure redundancy).

Out-of-lane note (defer detail to critic-logic): the read-modify-write of `_local` across get()/set() has no lock, so it is not thread-safe under concurrent access and any future bounded-eviction policy would also be racy.
